draft.introduction: Blue Cliff Record Case 1: Emperor Wu asks Bodhidharma what merit his religious works have earned. “No merit whatsoever.” What is the highest meaning of the holy truths? “Vast emptiness, nothing holy.”

translation.text: Emperor Wu of Liang asked Bodhidharma: “I have built temples, copied sutras, and ordained monks beyond number. What merit have I gained?” Bodhidharma said: “No merit whatsoever.” The Emperor asked: “What is the highest meaning of the holy truths?” Bodhidharma said: “Vast emptiness, nothing holy.” The Emperor said: “Who is this standing before me?” Bodhidharma said: “I don’t know.”
translation.original_text: 梁武帝問達磨大師、如何是圣諧第一义、磨云、廓然無圣

translator_notes.note: Bodhidharma’s triple negation—no merit, nothing holy, I don’t know—demolishes the three pillars of conventional religion: moral achievement, sacred doctrine, and personal identity. What remains is not nihilism but the “vast emptiness” that precedes all categories.
